column6:
There were 40 mcq  questions in the 1st round.
They were divided into 2 sections : 20 questions were from aptitude and logical reasoning and 20 were technical questions.
The aptitutde and logical reasoning questions were from easy to medium difficulty.
The technical questions were mostly from c and c++, they had code snippets which were based on core language and oops and questions from dbms were also there.
column8:
There were 2 rounds of interviews and both of them were one-on-one interviews.
For the first round, I firstly introduced myself and then I was asked my favourite subjects in college  and was asked questions according to that. The first question I was asked was that which algorithm is used by google maps to give the best shortest possible route(A* algorithm and djikstra's algorithm) and then I was asked to write some dbms queries and asked to explain joins and some questions from computer networks.
It was not the matter of how much you studied the previous night, all they tested is how much knowledge you actually have as they switched randomly from one subject to the other and even if you don't know everything, just explain the basic idea of what they are asking.
For the second round, it was supposed to be an hr round but the only hr question I was asked was tell me about yourself and then they drilled a lot on my technical knowledge, they asked why python is becoming very famous nowadays(the answer he expected was because python is open source and cross platform) and then I was asked about my projects and was asked to draw a schema based on a given scenario in such a way that writing the queries would be pretty neat and easy( just include more foreign keys) and then I was asked to make a class in java which represents a meeting room and was asked questions on threading and traffic management. They'll mostly stick to the projects and ask questions around that

column6:
First round was apti round which was easy but accuracy mattered a lot. 20apti questions, 10 from SQL and 10 from OOPS."
Next round was OA which had 5 DSA ques (2easy, 2medium and 1 hard) mostly based on string, array, map, matrix
column8:
Round 1: Technical Interview (55-60 minutes)
Started with introductory exchanges, followed by an in-depth discussion on my projects for about 20 minutes. After that some Key questions included: differences between SQL and NoSQL databases, selection sort with its space and time complexities, three data structure questions (two on ArrayList, one on HashMap), a real-life example and code of inheritance and polymorphism in Java, and three SQL queries (two manageable, one challenging, focused on joins and subqueries). The interview concluded with a reasoning puzzle and a discussion on my solutions in the online assessment. Most questions required me to write code or pseudocode on paper and explain my thought process.

Round 2: Technical + Managerial Interview (35-40 minutes)
Focused extensively on my first project, specifically the implementation of authentication and session management and the DB design of my project. I was given a problem statement on traffic management and asked to design a project for that and i  proposed a machine learning-based solution. Additional questions included 2-3 SQL-related queries and behavioral questions about my strengths and weaknesses.

column6: The aptitude round included a variety of straightforward questions covering topics such as time and work, pipes and cisterns, blood relations, and age-related problems.
column8:
In this interview experience, there were two rounds, both primarily focused on technical aspects.

The first round was a technical deep dive and lasted almost an hour. The interviewer asked detailed questions about my projects and concepts from object-oriented programming (OOP). I was required to write code snippets to demonstrate my understanding of OOP concepts, and I explained the principles behind them. Following this, the interviewer shifted to data structures and algorithms (DSA), where I was tested on various DSA topics. One of the questions involved parsing a string that contained patterns like "[MOD1] ABC [MOD2] XYZ " The task was to extract the substrings within the parentheses and return them as a collection, such as a list in Python or ArrayList in Java
Output: ["MOD1","MOD2"]

Next, I was asked to solve a star pattern problem where I had to generate a triangle pattern of stars. The first row would have one star, followed by three stars in the next row, and so on, with spaces properly aligned for each row. I had to write the code on paper, walk the interviewer through the logic, and dry run the solution to explain what was happening at each step. Towards the end of the round, the interviewer moved to SQL questions, focusing on basic concepts like joins and other simple SQL operations.

After some time, I was called for the second round, which had a more managerial focus. In this round, I was required to apply my technical knowledge to practical situations. The interviewer provided scenarios where I had to implement data structures and algorithms to solve real-world problems. This round aimed to test how well I could translate theoretical knowledge into practical applications.
